subject: Chemistry
q:
Identify the correct statement for B2H6 from those given below.

(A) In B2H6, all B-H bonds are equivalent.

(B) In B2H6, there are four 3-centre- 2-electron bonds.

(C) B2H6 is a Lewis acid.

(D) B2H6 can be synthesized from both BF3 and NaBH4.

(E) B2H6 is a planar molecule.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Identify the correct statement for B2H6 from those given below.

(A) In B2H6, all B-H bonds are equivalent.

(B) In B2H6, there are four 3-centre- 2-electron bonds.

(C) B2H6 is a Lewis acid.

(D) B2H6 can be synthesized from both BF3 and NaBH4.

(E) B2H6 is a planar molecule.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
solution:
B2H6 have four 2c-2e bonds and two 3c-2e bonds. Bridging bonds have larger bond length than terminal bonds. Angle between terminal bonds is more than angle between bridging bonds if all 4 terminal bonds are in one plane then bridging bonds are in perpendicular plane.

3NaBH4+4BF3⟶ether450 K3NaBF4+2B2H6

Therefore we can say that only statement (C) and (D) are correct.

q:
Consider the following elements $\mathrm{In}, \mathrm{Tl}, \mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{Pb}, \mathrm{Sn}$ and Ge .
The most stable oxidation states of elements with highest and lowest first ionisation enthalpies, respectively, are
question:
Consider the following elements $\mathrm{In}, \mathrm{Tl}, \mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{Pb}, \mathrm{Sn}$ and Ge .
The most stable oxidation states of elements with highest and lowest first ionisation enthalpies, respectively, are
solution:
Among $\mathrm{Al}, \mathrm{In}, \mathrm{Tl}, \mathrm{Ge}, \mathrm{Sn}, \mathrm{Pb}$, the metal having highest $\mathrm{IE}_1$ is Ge and lowest $\mathrm{IE}_1$ is In .
Most stable oxidation state of Ge is +4 and In is +3 .

q: Borazine, also known as inorganic benzene, can be prepared by the reaction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-equivalents of "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>" with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math>-equivalents of "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>". "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>" and "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>", respectively are
question: Borazine, also known as inorganic benzene, can be prepared by the reaction of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn></math>-equivalents of "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>" with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math>-equivalents of "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>". "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>" and "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math>", respectively are
solution:
Reaction of ammonia with diborane gives initially B2H6.2NH3 which is formulated as BH2NH32+ BH4- ; further heating gives borazine, B3N3H6 known as ''inorganic benzene'' in view of its ring structure with alternate BH and NH groups.

3B2H6+6NH3→3BH2NH32+BH4-→Heat2B3N3H6+12H2

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I: Group 13 trivalent halides get easily hydrolysed by water due to their covalent nature.

Statement II: AlCl3 upon hydrolysis in acidified aqueous solution forms octahedral AlH2O63+ ion.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :

Statement I: Group 13 trivalent halides get easily hydrolysed by water due to their covalent nature.

Statement II: AlCl3 upon hydrolysis in acidified aqueous solution forms octahedral AlH2O63+ ion.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is true but statement II is false
Statement I is false but statement II is true
Both statement I and statement II are false
Both statement I and statement II are true
solution:
In trivalent state most of the 13th group compounds being covalent are hydrolysed in water. Trichlorides of group 13 on hydrolysis in water form tetrahedral MOH4− species, the hybridisation state of element M is sp3.

In case of aluminium, acidified aqueous solution forms octahedral AlH2O63+ ion, which has an aluminium as a central metal atom and water molecules as ligands. The aluminium is in +3  oxidation state and forms 6 sp3d2 hybrid orbitals. 

Hence option D is the answer.

q: Diborane <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> reacts independently with <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> to produce, respectively:
question: Diborane <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">B</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>6</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> reacts independently with <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></math> and <math><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi></math> to produce, respectively:
solution:
Diborane reacts with oxygen exothermically to form boric oxide (B2O3)
B2H6+3O2→B2O3+3H2O
Diborane gets readily hydrolysed to give boric acid
B2H6+3H2O→2BOH3+3H2(g)

subject: Chemistry
q:
The correct statements from the following are :
A. Ionic radii of trivalent cations of group 13 elements decreases down the group.
B. Electronegativity of group 13 elements decreases down the group.
C. Among the group 13 elements, Boron has highest first ionisation enthalpy.
D. The trichloride and triiodide of group 13 elements are covalent in nature.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
The correct statements from the following are :
A. Ionic radii of trivalent cations of group 13 elements decreases down the group.
B. Electronegativity of group 13 elements decreases down the group.
C. Among the group 13 elements, Boron has highest first ionisation enthalpy.
D. The trichloride and triiodide of group 13 elements are covalent in nature.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
Analyzing statements about Group 13 elements (B, Al, Ga, In, Tl):
A. Ionic radii of M³⁺ cations: Down a group, more electron shells are added, so ionic radii increase, not decrease. FALSE
B. Electronegativity: Generally decreases down the group, though Ga shows an anomaly with higher electronegativity than Al. The statement is approximately true but has exceptions.
C. First ionization enthalpy: B (800.6 kJ/mol) is highest due to smallest size and highest nuclear charge density. Al (577.5), Ga (579), In (558), Tl (589). TRUE
D. Trichlorides and triodides: BCl₃, AlCl₃, GaCl₃, etc. are all covalent compounds (electron-deficient). BI₃, AlI₃, GaI₃, etc. are also covalent (I⁻ is large and polarizable). TRUE
The clearly correct statements are C and D.

subject: Chemistry
q:
It is noticed that $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$ is more stable than $\mathrm{Pb}^{4+}$ but $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}$ is less stable than $\mathrm{Sn}^{4+}$. Observe the following reactions.
$\mathrm{PbO}_{2}+\mathrm{Pb} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{PbO} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}(1)$
$\mathrm{SnO}_{2}+\mathrm{Sn} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SnO} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}(2)$
Identify the correct set from the following
question:
It is noticed that $\mathrm{Pb}^{2+}$ is more stable than $\mathrm{Pb}^{4+}$ but $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}$ is less stable than $\mathrm{Sn}^{4+}$. Observe the following reactions.
$\mathrm{PbO}_{2}+\mathrm{Pb} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{PbO} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}(1)$
$\mathrm{SnO}_{2}+\mathrm{Sn} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SnO} ; \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^{\circ}(2)$
Identify the correct set from the following
solution:
The stability of oxidation states in Group 14 is governed by the inert pair effect, which increases down the group.
For Lead ($Pb$), the $+2$ oxidation state is more stable than the $+4$ state ($Pb^{2+} > Pb^{4+}$).
In the reaction $PbO_2 + Pb \rightarrow 2PbO$, $Pb^{4+}$ is being reduced to $Pb^{2+}$ and $Pb^0$ is being oxidized to $Pb^{2+}$. Since the product contains the more stable oxidation state, the reaction is spontaneous, meaning $\Delta_r G^\circ(1) < 0$.
For Tin ($Sn$), the $+4$ oxidation state is more stable than the $+2$ state ($Sn^{4+} > Sn^{2+}$).
In the reaction $SnO_2 + Sn \rightarrow 2SnO$, the stable $Sn^{4+}$ is being converted into the less stable $Sn^{2+}$. This process is non-spontaneous, meaning $\Delta_r G^\circ(2) > 0$.
Thus, the correct set is $\Delta_r G^\circ(1) < 0$ and $\Delta_r G^\circ(2) > 0$.

q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Boron is extremely hard indicating its high lattice energy.

Statement II: Boron has highest melting and boiling point compared to its other group members.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement I: Boron is extremely hard indicating its high lattice energy.

Statement II: Boron has highest melting and boiling point compared to its other group members.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect
Both statement I and Statement II are correct
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct
solution:
Boron is extremely hard as it exists as B12 icosahedral molecular units which accounts for its high lattice energy. So, statement-I is correct.

Due to small atomic size boron forms strong covalent bonds with the neighbouring atoms. Thus, boron atoms are closely packed in its solid, thus boron has high melting and boiling points. So, statement-II is also correct.

q: The correct order of bond enthalpy (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>) is:
question: The correct order of bond enthalpy (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>kJ</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>–</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>) is:
solution:
As bond length increases, bond energy decreases.

Bond Bond energy (kJ mol-1)
C - C 348
Si - Si 297
Ge - Ge 260
Sn - Sn 240

So, bond enthalpy order C - C > Si - Si > Ge - Ge > Sn - Sn

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : The number of pairs among $\left[\mathrm{SiO}_{2}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{SnO}, \mathrm{SnO}_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{PbO}, \mathrm{PbO}_{2}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{GeO}, \mathrm{GeO}_{2}\right]$, which contain oxides that are both amphoteric is 2.
Statement II: $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ is an electron deficient molecule, can act as a Lewis acid, forms adduct with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and has a trigonal planar geometry.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements :
Statement I : The number of pairs among $\left[\mathrm{SiO}_{2}, \mathrm{CO}_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{SnO}, \mathrm{SnO}_{2}\right],\left[\mathrm{PbO}, \mathrm{PbO}_{2}\right]$ and $\left[\mathrm{GeO}, \mathrm{GeO}_{2}\right]$, which contain oxides that are both amphoteric is 2.
Statement II: $\mathrm{BF}_{3}$ is an electron deficient molecule, can act as a Lewis acid, forms adduct with $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and has a trigonal planar geometry.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Statement I is false but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I: Checking amphoteric nature of oxide pairs:
$[SiO_2, CO_2]$: Both acidic (non-metal oxides)
$[SnO, SnO_2]$: Both amphoteric
$[PbO, PbO_2]$: $PbO$ is amphoteric but $PbO_2$ is predominantly acidic/oxidizing
$[GeO, GeO_2]$: Both amphoteric (Ge is metalloid)
2 pairs have both oxides amphoteric. Statement I is true.
Statement II: $BF_3$ has B with 6 valence electrons (electron deficient), acts as Lewis acid, forms $BF_3 \cdot NH_3$ adduct, has $sp^2$ hybridization giving trigonal planar geometry. Statement II is true.

X is:
question: A group 13 element <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></msup><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">'</mi></msup></math> reacts with chlorine gas to produce a compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>XCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mo>.</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mi>XCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> is electron deficient and easily reacts with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>to</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi>form</mi><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><msub><mi>Cl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>←</mo><msub><mi>NH</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> adduct; however, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>XCl</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> does not dimerize. X is:
solution:
BCl3


 

Boron react with chlorine gas to produce compound BCl3 which is electron deficient. It does not dimerize because of small boron atom. It react with ammonia to form aduct. In it ammonia act as lewis base and BCl3 act as lewis acid.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Melting point of Boron (2453 K) is unusually high in group 13 elements.
Reason (R) : Solid Boron has very strong crystalline lattice.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below ;
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Melting point of Boron (2453 K) is unusually high in group 13 elements.
Reason (R) : Solid Boron has very strong crystalline lattice.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below ;
options: Both (A) and (R) are correct but (R) Is not the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
solution:
Boron is non-metallic in nature. It is extremely hard and black coloured solid. Due to a very strong crystalline lattice, boron has an unusually high melting point. The rest of the members are soft metals with a low melting point and high electrical conductivity.

Hence, Solid Boron has very strong crystalline lattice, so its melting point is unusually high in group 13 elements.

Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)

question:
Given below are two statements: one is labelled as Assertion A and the other is labelled as Reason R

Assertion A: Boron is unable to form BF63-

Reason R: Size of B is very small.

In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below
options: Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are true and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>

Both <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> are true but <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">R</mi></math> is not the correct explanation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></math>
solution:
Due to non-availability of d orbitals, boron is unable to expand its octet. Therefore, the maximum covalence of boron cannot exceed 4.

Boron is the first element from top in group 13 and the atomic size increases down the group. Therefore, boron is smallest in size.

Assertion A: True

Reason R: True but not correct explanation.

subject: Chemistry
q:
Given below are two statements:

Statement (I) : SiO2 and GeO2 are acidic while SnO and PbO are amphoteric in nature.
Statement (II) : Allotropic forms of carbon are due to property of catenation and pπ-dπ bond formation.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
Given below are two statements:

Statement (I) : SiO2 and GeO2 are acidic while SnO and PbO are amphoteric in nature.
Statement (II) : Allotropic forms of carbon are due to property of catenation and pπ-dπ bond formation.

In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are false
Both Statement I and Statement II are true
Statement I is true but Statement II is false
Statement I is true but Statement II is true
solution:
Statement I,

SnO, PbO, SnO2, PbO2 : Amphoteric CO2, SiO2, GeO2, GeO : Acidic CO : Neutral

According to Statement II,
Carbon does not have d-orbitals so can not form pπ-dπ Bond with itself. Due to properties of catenation and pπ-pπ bond formation, carbon is able to show allotropic forms. Allotropic forms of carbon can be crystalline and amorphous.
Diamond and graphite are two well-known crystalline forms of carbon. 

Hence option C is the answer.
